summary: O'Neil argues that TikTok's recommendation algorithm, like all recommendation engines, can be manipulated by whoever controls it to amplify or suppress any type of content. She points out that this capability to shape what users see is inherent to how recommendation systems work. Notably, she clarifies that the ability to manipulate these algorithms isn't inherently negative—it can be used for beneficial purposes as well as harmful ones.
key_points: Recommendation engine controllers have the power to emphasize or suppress any content they choose
TikTok's algorithm operates like other recommendation engines in this regard
The ability to manipulate recommendation algorithms is actually a feature, not a bug—it can serve good purposes
The real question is who controls the algorithm and what their intentions are
money_quotes: whoever controls TikTok's algorithm can of course tamp down or emphasize whatever kind of content they want, misinformation or otherwise
being able to manipulate recommendation algorithms is in general a good thing!
it can be used for good or for evil
key_insight: The power to manipulate recommendation algorithms is neither good nor bad—what matters is who wields that power and toward what ends.
